F Lustre is that this slide training, the topic is looping, which is a back-up of the sale, the strategy that allows me to close anyone who's closeable after they say let me think about it. And I have to say that if there is one indicator for a salesperson, and for that matter entrepreneur or a negotiator, but really for a salesperson, the amount of money that you make at the end of the day is going to be directly related to how many people that you can get who are sitting on the fence to let me think about it, let me call your back, spare time of year, got to speak to my wife, right? It's getting those people to ultimately say yes that dictates how much money you make. You know, any monkey can read through a script seriously and just read through a sales script and get a lay down client. So it's like, yeah, yeah, great. Oh my God, great. They're with you the whole way through, right? And they just say yes when you ask for the order. That's easy. Anyone can do that. The reason that salespeople make so much money and it's the highest paid profession out there on average is because that salespeople can get people who are on the fence to say yes. You get it? That's the difference. And that's what the straight line is all about. So the topic today specifically, what happens after the client says let me think about it or let me call your back, spare time of year, got to talk to my wife, my butcher, the baker, the candlestick baker, you get it? What happens then? Now I hit on the strategy back in 1988, believe it or not. And I've been refining it ever since and it's so powerful that it can be manipulative. You don't do it the right way, meaning if you're not using your ethics and integrity here, it's manipulative. So I want you to really make sure that you maintain your ethics and integrity and you use this strategy because it's fucking powerful and it really, really works. And that is, you know, what is a fucking webinar? I think the whole webinar world is completely broken. I think it's a fucking joke. When you think about it, you know, I get paid a lot of money to travel around the world and I'll go into a conference center or a stadium and get eight, 10,000 people, at least 5,000 people, right? And I get paid a couple hundred thousand dollars, maybe more sometimes if I'm lucky, right? And that's a great way to make money, right? But when you think about the inefficiency of that, people have to drive to a place, I've got to fly to a place with so much money spent on a venue, on lighting, on sell this shit, right? Yet we can, right now, I can literally, with losing nothing, losing no information, I can talk to you in real time, I can answer your questions, I can give you massive value, right? And that's what a webinar is supposed to be, right? I mean, where I can essentially, a seminar on the web. But what's happened is, it's turned into this fucking slugfest right now, where all these webinars are about, is to sell you shit. Topic of the day is looping. And let me just go very slowly here because I'm really going to dig into this in detail. The straight line system is based on one overarching principle. OK. And let me tell you what it is. It's that at the end of the day, sales is based on the transfer of emotion. That's what sales is all about. Your transferring emotion and the primary emotion that you're transferring is the emotion of certainty, certainty about your product. Your product is awesome. Right. There's certainty that you are a great person. They want to deal with you. So there's a have to love the product and also trust and connect with you. So there's certainty about yourself and there's certainty about the company that you work for. I mean, the company that stands behind the product. In other words, people are not going to buy your product if they don't trust you, the salesperson or the company that stands behind the product. They just simply will not buy from you. They'll say, let me think about it, let me call you back. And that's what the objections are all about. In other words, objections are a smoke screen for uncertainty. This is the basis of what allowed me to take people who are not great salespeople. People with no ability and turning into world class closest. So is that concept. And then I figured out a way to essentially use that concept to show them how to transfer certainty. In other words, for someone like myself who's a natural born closer, I never had to consider this automatically. My brain was wired in a certain way where when I'd speak to people and sell them, I would say things and use tonality and buy them. I would do it in a way that transferred certainty to my prospect. And I wouldn't just talk about my product. Intuitively, I also knew how to talk about myself, sell myself and the company that stands behind the product. In some level, I had this ability as a natural born closest to walk in the prospect shoes and say what I want to hear if I was the other person. And I would just say that to them and it happened in real time. And that's what made me a great salesman for the time I was literally old enough to talk, but really manifested itself when it's my early 20s. And I started my first business and I just blew out all the numbers. We get the top salesmen in the world. It was this ability that was innate to transfer certainty and to know that there were three types of certainty. Well, this crystallized when I tried to take 12 guys who worked for me selling stocks to call rich people and they couldn't do it. And I realized there was something missing from this equation. And I came up with a system called the straight line, which a lot of you guys and most of you know about by now, right? And I began teaching the system and it worked like magic. It took people who had no business being millionaires and made them into millionaires because it turned out to be far more than just sales. It turned out to be a communication strategy. It showed people how to get any thought, any idea, any hope, any dream across to another human being or group of people. And there is no more powerful skill when it comes to making money. OK, so going back now to this idea of uncertainty and certainty, remember what's happening here. Let me just give you this one more second. I'll explain how it relates to looping. When a prospect enters into a sales encounter, they are going to be at a far lower level of certainty than the salesperson. Let's just say they better be. The job as the salesperson, your job is to enter that encounter, that stand absolute certainty. And then what you do is you transfer that certainty to the prospect. So you're absolutely certain the prospect is far less. You transfer that certainty, right? So what happens is that when you do that and you go through the sales process and you ask for the order for the first time, guess what? Most of the time, like 90% of the time, 95% of the time, the prospects are still not at a high enough level of certainty. They're not there yet. They're not 100% sure that your product is the best, that it's going to do everything you said it would do. It's going to meet all their needs, remove their pain. They're not 100% sure they can trust you. They probably just met you. Maybe they didn't see you selling on the phone or through the internet. No one's even seen you. So they don't trust you at a high enough level and don't trust the company that stands behind the product at a high enough level. Now, in the straight line, we refer to these things as the three tens. We call them the three tens. And the idea here is a continuum of certainty with the one being that someone's uncertain and a 10 being their absolute loser. So this is they love your product. This is they hate their product. This is they absolutely trust you. This is they think you're a fucking line scumbag. Here they love the company, they trust the company, the company's a lapse. They we don't trust they're going to rip our money. They're going to take our information and sell it. They probably give us good customer service. That's what a one is. So a 10 means they love it and a one means they hate it. Your goal as you move someone down the straight line from the open to the close, you try to get them to a 10, 10, 10, meaning a 10 about the product, a 10 about yourself, a 10 about the company, right? And then corral those three tens and ask for the order. And if you do that successfully, let me just turn the picture. OK, if you do that successfully, guess what? You have a good shot of closing the sale. If you have it, it can pick it. They're really sure they love the product. They're really sure about you and they're really sure about the company that stands behind the product, an excellent shot of closing, right? But what happens is, is that it's very difficult to do that in one shot. So now I'm going to draw the straight line out for you. Watch, this is your opening. Right here where the sale begins and this is your close, right? And your job as an influencer, as a salesperson is you trying to push the person down the straight line towards the close. Every word you say, everything you do, every question you ask, every document you show, whatever your sales tools are, right? You're trying to move them down the straight line coming open to the close to this ultra high level of certainty, which point they say yes. Right? So that's what the perfect straight line sale looks like. Whatever you say, everything you do, they're right on one. But what typically happens is your prospects try to give off the line. They want to spiral out. They have questions, they have objections. They talk about things that are not germane to the sale. You want to keep them on the line so you have these healthy boundaries above and below the line. The key to successful selling is to stay inside these values. In control, in control, ad control, ad control. Here's where you're spiraling off the Pluto or Uranus, not good places to be for most of us, it leaves, right? So your job against someone, you have to understand this to understand what looping does, right? So this is your job. Your job is to slowly but surely move the prospect down the straight line. Now, when you ask to the order for the first time, meaning you go through your presentation, and you say, here's what I'd like you to do, pick up this X, Y, Z prospect, whatever it might be. See, at the point when you ask for the order for the first time, that's when you're ready to roll up your sleeves and make the sale work. Most people, listen, what can they say here? What are the three responses they can say? If you ask for the order, they can say, yes, let's do it, which means you have a lay down sale. They say, just put right on the gate, no objections. Awesome, very rare, maybe one side of every 100 sales or maybe twice, very low percentage. They could say no, meaning not my interest, I don't want it. And that's just as rare, even more rare, by the way. And maybe turns out to be about 97% of the time. And what maybe represents is all the common objections. Let me think about it, let me call you back there time of year. They're smoke screens for uncertainty because if they really loved your product and if they totally trusted you and they knew the company would stand behind the product, would they say let me know? Now, they don't say that to you, but that's what's going on inside the head. So they say, let me think about it. So I know that this is what's really going on. That's what they said. Question, how do I close the gap between the two? In other words, I know that if I can get them to a 10, 10, 10 in a single moment in time and ask for the order the right way, I got an excellent shot of closing. I know that history proves that that I can close almost anyone who's closeable if I get into a 10, 10, 10 in a single moment in time and ask for the order the right way and I'll explain how to ask for the order afterwards. OK. So that's the truth. That's a basic truth. What do I do? Well, what I'm going to do is I'm actually going to go about this in a very scientific, systematic way and I call it picking the lock of their buying combination. In other words, human beings will like, well, it's like a safe. You have a crack the safe, right? And there are all together five numbers in the safe. If you can line up all five of these numbers, you got an excellent 99 percent chance of closing it. You crack all five numbers. So I'm like a safe cracker in a sense. So what does a safe cracker do when a safe? Let's say there's five numbers, actually there's five numbers in the combination. The first three numbers are number one, they must love your product. That's the first number in the combination. You have to get to a 10. They love your product. Second number in the combination, they must trust and connect with you at a really high level. That's the second 10. Third 10, they must trust and connect with the company. 10, 10, 10. Those are the first three numbers in the buying combination. And I'll get to the last two in about 15, 20 minutes from that when we're done with these three. OK. So that's what I'm doing. So how does a safe cracker go about cracking the safe? We do it one number at a time. And what we do with the straight line is always start to what makes it so beautiful that every sale is the same. We always handle it in exactly the same way. So when you watch here. OK. This is your open and that's your close. This is your open. This is your close. Right. We move down the straight line to the close. You're at this point in the sale. You have your values here and you have your values here. Right. They say, let me think about it. Right. And when they say, let me think about it, that's your first objection. So all I'm going to do right now is all I want to find out is where do they stand on the certainty scale for my product? In other words, that's all I want to know right now. I get this other things going on here. But what I need to know before I can do anything intelligent right now to close the sale, I must understand where are they on the certainty? Are they at a one? Are they at a pay or somewhere in between? They're probably not at a one because they wouldn't be on the call anymore. Right. And they're certainly not at a pay at this point. They probably would be a lot more enthusiastic to buy. Right. So what I do is I take this objection. Let me think about it. Right. Let me think about it. Let me think about it. Right. And I engage in a process called deflection. Deflection. What is deflection? Deflection means that I don't answer the objection head on. I deflect it and I take back control of the sale. So watch what happens. So it sounds good. Jordan, let us for the order they say, it sounds good. Jordan, let me think about it. Here's how deflection works. I say, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? You understand? I said, I hear what you're saying. So I don't break the point. I hear what you're saying. My tone out is reasonable. I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. And now the tone out is money aside. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? That's deflection. So I took, let me think about it. And now I'm asking him a question, meaning how do you feel about the first 10? I mean, I should think, do you like the idea? Does it make sense to you? Because here's the deal. If they don't like my product, they're not going to buy it. And what I see all the time when I'm watching untrained salespeople is they will try to close someone who's not really that crazy about their product. In other words, they gave a sales presentation to someone. Right? It means this total presentation. Like a hit lead objection. And I'm listening because I'm training this before I start training them. Right? So I'm listening to the call and then try to close this person. It's very obvious that the person is still not sure if they even think the product is right for them. And yet the salesperson is still trying to close them. Just think of the sheer fucking lunacy of a bad. You try to close someone who doesn't even really like your product that much. What's the point of that? It's a waste of time. So what I do is I take that control of the sale and I say, I hear what you're saying. But let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Now, obviously if I'm working, let's say I'm selling TVs or appliances. I'm not going to say to someone, but let me ask you a question. Does the TV make sense to you? Do you like to say what the fuck is wrong with this guy? Right? So you have to be elegant here. But I've trained the largest appliance company in Australia, Harvey Norman. Right? And I increase their clothes with 30% in one training. So how would you do that? And that's so after you gave your presentation, someone said, let me see. I said, let me ask you what do you think of the TV? Is this you like this one? Is this the right one? What do you think? How does this grab you? Just ask the question. You have to say it makes sense to you. But you say, what do you think of this? You know what? You know, I understand what you think of the TV. You like that? You let the right one feel and let them speak. And what they'll say is, yeah, it sounds pretty good. Well, yeah, it looks pretty good. What is that on the certainty scale? So I say, do you like the idea they say? Yeah, it sounds pretty good. Is that a 10? This is the magic pipe right here. This is where the magic happens. They say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. Where is that? It's not a 10. That's like a five or a six. So it says, oh, yeah, it sounds pretty good. That's their answer. Where's that? That's it. They say, oh, yeah, no, it's awesome. I love this TV. You want this call? Oh, that's stock. Yes. Oh, I love the idea. That's a 10. Well, they say, yes, man, that's all right. That's a four of love. So when I ask that question, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? The response I get back from the prospect, both the words and even more important, the tonality, show me where they are on the certainty scale. That's the key. That's what I'm doing here. So let's say they say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. Is it a six? Well, is someone going to buy from me when they're at a six? That's just barely certain. You're like men's amends, right? That's not even close enough to have someone buy from you. Right? So what do you do? You have to increase their certainty. So what I do, I say, so what I'll do is watch. I'll say exactly. It really is a great idea. So they say, yeah, it sounds pretty. I'll say exactly. It really is a great idea. In fact, the true beauty of the situation. And now watch what I do. This is what why is called looping. So they I asked for the order of the first time and they say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. And then they say, let me think about it. I say, well, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And their response is, yeah, it sounds pretty good. That's a six. So I'm going to say exactly. And I use my tonality and enter their world. I don't say, yeah, exactly. I'm essentially matched their tone out and their enthusiasm. I say exactly. It really is a great idea. In fact, the true beauty is and then I loop back into the front half of the sale and I make a follow up presentation that picks up on my initial presentation left off. You get that? In other words, the key to overcoming objection, so to speak, well, is to essentially loop around them. So when it's your objection, you know, the smoke screen for these three tens. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to essentially loop back. I'm going to go back into presentation mode and I'm going to re are a re present the idea to them a second time. So to do that effectively, I need to have prepared myself before. So before I enter any sale, this will discuss this in more detail how you do this. When we get we talk another week, we talk about scripting and language patterns. But the point is I have already prepared not just initial presentation. I have my initial presentation, which frames my product and frames the benefits. And now I have a secondary presentation. I've already prepared a tertiary third one I prepared. So I know that when they said, let me think about it. I have two more great language patterns to throw at them to change their mind. In other words, they say they're not that certain. So I'm OK. Let me give you some more information now to make you certain. So essentially I construct my presentation not to throw every benefit, every feature. I don't kitchen sick them as the phrase goes. I frame why my product is awesome in the front end. I ask for the order knowing I'll probably get hit with an objection. And then when I do get it, I loop back into the sale and I hit them with a follow up pattern that picks up right where my initial presentation left off. And this one's even more powerful. It's got you know, stream from the whole top's logic, right? And basically I raise their level of certainty now through the second pattern. So at the end of the second pattern, I'm always in by saying, you see what I'm saying here? Really, it makes sense to you now. I was like, oh, yeah, 99% of the time they will. So by looping backwards and giving them a follow up presentation. And then I asked the same question again. Watch, I said, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And they said, yeah, it sounds pretty good. And now I looped back. I made a follow presentation. I asked the same question again. Do you like the idea? I was like, oh my God, I love. Yes, Jordan. Yes. Now I get it. That's the power of your first loop. So now when you ask the question again, they say, oh, yeah, they're probably annoying now. Now you have a shot of closing them before they were too low on the certain scale. I'm going to put a little great in my mouth. Give my mouth a noise. Awesome. OK. So you have a great feel for it. Take a great yourself. OK. Everyone getting this. So while I had no shot of closing them before, because they weren't certain about my product, now I have an excellent shot of close ones. I got them to say, yeah, no, I like, yo, it's great. I get it. Yeah, it's a great product. Bing. Now, when I ask for the order at this point, yes or no, the answer is no, I will not. Why? Because I still have more work to do. There are two more tennis here. That was the first time. Check. Now I have to increase their level of certainty about me, the sales person. So I'll say so they'll say, oh, yeah, no, I love it. Yes, great job. I say exactly. It really is a great idea. Exactly. It's amazing TV. I said, let me ask you another question. Now I go into my next page. I call my forest companion. So if I've been your broker or agent over you and I have been working together for the last three or four years, and I've been making you money on a consistent basis, then you probably wouldn't be saying, let me think about it right now. You'd be saying, Jordan, pick me up a block of five thousand. So Jordan, yeah, let's take that insurance policy out right now. Jordan, of course, let's buy this investment property or whatever you're selling, it doesn't matter. But the point is, let me ask you this, you know, if you and I knew, here's the adjustment, if you and I knew each other and I've been selling you things for three or four years and everything I sold you had exceeded your expectations, I essentially under promise over the limit, your life was better off performing and you probably wouldn't be saying, let me think about it right now. Am I right? And when you say that to someone in a sales setting, 95% of the time they'll say, yeah, well, well, then I would. Because that's the truth. See what you've done now is something incredibly powerful. You reframed the sale from one of, let me think about it to, I don't really trust you. In other words, if you knew me, if you really knew everything about me, how hard I worked for my clients or the degrees I had, how great I am at helping you make money or saving money or filling a certain need or fixing your pipes or doing your life, you really knew how great, how much my clients loved me, how awesome I was and how much attention the details. You knew me, you wouldn't be saying, let me think about it. Am I right? And they'll say, oh yeah, well, of course, Jordan, well, then I would and I say exactly and that I can understand. You don't know me. I don't have the luxury of a track record. And now I've set myself up to run my forest gump patterns. Now, for those of you who read the book, the forest, you know what this is. Let me just very quickly tell you what the forest gump pattern is. It goes back to the movie forest. Come to my favorite movies. I'm sure you've all seen it. It's when forest is five or six years old, you're at the bus stop for his first day of school, the bus pulls up, right? And forest is like this. He's doing what forest does. He's standing in space like a forest, right? And the bus drivers opens the doors and forest looks at her. It says gruff moon, a cigarette in her mouth. And he freezes like a deer in the head. Like doesn't get on the bus. So she says, well, are you getting on the bus? He goes, my mom had told me not to talk to strangers, right? And the woman starts to realize what she's dealing with. So she softens her tone and she says to young forest as well. This is the bus to school. And then forest is unmoved by that because it doesn't solve his problem. He doesn't know this person. He doesn't trust this person. So there's a kind of deadlock there. She was well, are you getting on? And finally, forest figures out a way to break the deadlock. He goes, well, my name is forest forest gump. And he goes, well, I'm Dorothy. I'm your bus driver. And he gets on the fucking bus. Now, as simple as that example is, it's really fucking profound. See, if you want someone to trust you, what do you do? Tell them that you're trustworthy. Tell them who you are. Tell them your name. And they probably forgot your fucking name with this one. You probably just, you know, you probably could be on the phone. They don't know your name, right? Say, let me say now that I can understand. You don't know me. I don't have a track record. Let me take a moment to reintroduce myself. My name is Jordan Belforda, John Smith. I'm the managing director of XYZ company. I pride myself on this. I do all this is what I do for my clients. I go to the equity. And then what you're going to do in the same way that you wrote a script for your product, you want to write two or three patterns about yourself. Tell them about yourself. Tell them how hard you work, that you plan on being the top agent in your company, that you work on repeat business. You're not looking to get rich or anyone transaction. You'll be there for them in good times and they will have a come up with whatever is growing for your industry and for you. And at the end of that pattern, that my life raises your second 10. Bing, that's that's trusting you. That leaves our third 10. How do we handle that? Very simply at the end of that forest gum pattern, you add on these words. And as far as my company goes, I repeat those magic words that easy, right? So after you double your forest gum pattern, you go through the same, you say, why? You say, as far as my company goes. That's it. So you say, sorry, you end talking about yourself and I'll go the extra mile. And as far as my company goes, XYZ company, we are one of the most well respected and then you talk about your company. So these are your transition words from your forest gum pattern to now talking about the third 10. It's a my company doesn't want this award. We do we prize ourselves on that and tell them about their company. So what you've just done now is you've taken an objection right now. I'm taking an objection. So you take an objection on the words of this government. You take trees, right? So I'll change you straight on. These are over here, right? These are over here. Are you open? It's your clothes, right? Your dotted lines above and below the line. OK, so watch. You try to go down the line, right? They try to go off the line, right? You hit the first objection. You loop back into presentation mode where you sold the product, then you sold yourself, then you sold the company. And now you're going to ask for the order for second time. And now you ask for the order again. That's a loop. So let me repeat here. This is what a loop is. A loop is how you take any of the common objections and use it as an opportunity to build more and more certainty for each of the three tens and then you transition into a close. So it always starts a loop begins by answering someone's objection. You get hit with an objection. You know, OK, it's time to loop, right? Your first loop begins with the big D deflection. I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. This is the idea. Makes sense to you. Do you like the idea? That's deflection, right? That's the beginning of your first loop. And from there, you're going to resell the product, resell yourself, resell the company. And now you're going to ask for the order again a second time. That's what a loop does. And you do that without breaking rapport. And that's the key. See, virtually every other objection handling strategy I've ever seen. It's always about getting into a confrontation about why the customer is wrong and you're right, meaning why they should buy and why they shouldn't think about it rather than cutting through all the bullshit and looping back and creating massive certainty for the three tens and then transitioning into a close. That's why it's so powerful. Now, I told you there was three elements. We have about another 10 minutes here to go. 15 minutes to go. Good. All right. Any comment? How the polls going? But, but. Well, great. Yeah. I'm asking you to write the Q&A area. OK. We're going to give we'll do a Q&A in about seven minutes. I want to. There's two more numbers in the buying combination. Right. Let me go through real quick. For those tougher nuts to crack, because what you'll find here is that when you ask for the order of second time here, you'll run into a phenomenon called objection hopping. What is objection hopping? Objection hopping is that let me think about it. I'll say, oh, wow. I love it. Can I just call you back? So let me think about it. I can let me call you back. Or I just told my partner or it's not the right time right now. But I call it back. They hop to an objection. Now, when they do that, the reason typically is you may not quite high enough on the certainty sale. Now, remember there are two types of certainty. There's logical certainty and emotional certainty. So what you might have got them a logically certain, mean that it makes sense in purity, the features, the benefits, the value proposition, they say, yeah, it makes sense in their gut. They don't have to have it. They can't see themselves using the product and feeling great. That's what emotional certainty is about. You have to create that too. So what we do here is when we run a loop and a loop always ends with us asking for the order of second time or third time. Right. If you hit the second objection and almost always be a separate one, it'll be another little hop to a new one. The second time that you actually have to answer the objection. But the point is that the rebuttal, all the rebuttal does is give you the right to speak more. Let me explain. If someone says to you, I just got to call you back and I say, you know, John, I understand you want to call back. Now listen closely here. This is the rebuttal. John, I've been doing this for quite some time now. And it is one thing I've seen is that once almost to think about it and call you back, they almost never do. Not because they don't love the idea. What you're abutting, their objection about why they don't want to think about it. I have 30 of those and 30 for my wife and every other one. Because I've created them over the years. I created a rebuttal. But that rebuttal into itself is fucking worthless. That's not going to close. It's not going to say that and then ask for the order again and close the deal. That's an exercise in futility. What you have to do is you use that rebuttal and that gives you the right to speak more. So when you answer that objection with this rebuttal at the end of the rebuttal, so it'll be watch, I'd say people, you put it in the mind and you'll decide to guess it and I don't want you to do that. John, let me say this. Let me say I loop back again and I resell the first 10 again. And the second I'll go right to that process first 10, second 10, third 10. How the question was how do you handle objections when they come up in the beginning of the conversation before you even present? All right. So here's here's the story and you're going to love this. That was what. Instigated me creating the straight line, that very issue on the night that I invented the straight line, I stood in front of my 12 brokers, these first 12 strait knights in this old dilapidated boardroom. Right. And I said to them, I said, guys, if you know, why can't you close? I'm doing it. Danny's doing it. What the fuck is wrong with you? I know you guys. What's happening? And they're like, there's too many objections. There's thousands of objections. Like, oh, my. And they're like, we're getting barraged with objections. It's raining down on us. And someone goes, yeah, we can't even get a pitch off. And I'm like, what? Like, I can't get off pitch. We're getting interrupted. And I'm like, wait a second. I'm like, I don't get interrupted. And I and I'm like, what the fuck? Why is someone interrupting them before they even sell us? They've already said this. They've already said, guys, this. Let me just back up a second so you understand how ridiculous this is. So they would say to someone, sort of if you have 60 seconds, like to share an idea with you, you got a minute. And the guy would say, yeah, go ahead. You couldn't be in a pitch until you actually got. Say, yeah, sure, go ahead. What's like, tell me about the stock you recommended. And then eight seconds later, they're hitting you with an objection. You haven't asked to buy anything yet. What the fuck is that? It never happens to me ever, ever, never happened. And I realized the reason for it is because my salespeople, these young kids, they were not taking control of the sale. They were not being perceived the right way. This is a different topic for a different training. But the point was is that what's happening to you is an outgrowth of you. Not making that killer first impression in those first four seconds that allows you to take control of sale. See, you are what watch what that means when someone you're trying to present an idea and they interrupt you and take you off the straight line. That's what these values are about. Now, if they do that, you can't interrupt them. You can't say, whoa, whoa, stop, buddy. Don't give me an objection now because I'm a straight line salesperson. You're going off the fucking fluo. That's you can't do that. There's two things that are going to happen. Number one, a once you learn how to sound awesome using the right tonalities and person, the right body language and have the right words. You're saying in those first four seconds in the 10 seconds that follow. And the person in the person's mind, they say, wow, this person sounds pretty sharp. They sound they sound good. They're enthusiastic. They seem like they're an expert. Once that thought hits the other prospects brain, they stop interrupting you. They defer to you because when we're in the presence of experts, we defer, we let the expert control the flow. That's what happens. So you're seeing a symptom right now of not coming off as an expert shop, is attack enthusiastic and you're getting hit with objections as a result of that. Once you start using the right, that's why the front half of the straight line is so crucial because once you start executing the front line, this front half of the straight line, I promise you, those objections in the beginning will cease to exist. They really will. Yeah, once in a blue moon, you get hit with it and you're not a handle that because it's an easy way to handle that. Now you go through your second and your forest, come pattern and talk about the company, the next time you ask for the order, they're not going to say send me information on one of research. They're going to say it's a bad time of year. Don't hop to an image as proof that I'm right. Watch what happens if you run your first pattern correctly. They're not going to say to you it sounds good, but I still need to do more research that will lose that objection unless you're dealing at a really, really high ticket item where they really should do more research. In that case, you need to change your sale cycle and get some information in front of them before you actually ask for the order or do your sale over a real webinar where you educate someone and then ask for the order. That's a different story, right? But the point is, is that the function when someone says I need to do more research is the perfect example of a lack of certainty. I need to do more research so I can see if I should be more certain. You're going to say I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And they'll say it sounds pretty good. I'm going to say exactly. In fact, the true beauty is like, oh, that's what I forgot to tell you. Oh, the true beauty is let me tell you this and I come with a better reason why they should buy. Give me more information. And then I'm done it. I say, you see what I'm saying here, John? Does the idea make sense? I'll be like, oh, no, yeah, now I get it. Bingo, no longer do you need to do research because now you get it. Once they make that public admission, you're 80% of the way there. Then you got to reset yourself, the company and so forth and ask for the order. Let me just exit. I told you I would show you how to ask for the order. I also want to go through before we end this call. I want to go through something called the tri-tonal closing pattern. Now I'm going to stand up here and do it by the board. Watch it's when you're asking for the order, you do not want to go in a state of absolute certainty. So you say it and you say, John, and believe me, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Sound fair enough? So you're absolutely certain that you believe me, John, the only problem you'll have and then you're in this sort of calm, sincere tone. So absolute certainty collapses to utter sincerity is that you didn't buy more. Sound fair enough in your reasonable man tone. Absolute sincerity, absolute certainty, utter sincerity, reasonable man. There's three tones that go into closed. Do not close. John, is that sound fair enough? I was like, whoa, what the fuck? They won't buy, right? See and believe me, John, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Does that sound fair enough? Those three tonality put together after you run a loop. It's magical. I can't tell you how many thousands and thousands and thousands of sales I've seen closed first hand just because the salesman did that while I coached them actually. I stood over them and fed them those lines. They're like sound fair enough. They used to call it back and they joined. He dropped some fairy dust on me where I just walked by someone. They dropped fairy dust on him. OK, so that's the story. So guys, listen, this was an awesome train today. I went over by about five minutes. Right. Sign up for this. All right. If you don't, you're out of your frickin mind. It's nine dollars. I'm going to raise the price next week just so you know. But we'll be back next week for another live training and I'll see you then. All right, sweet. I'm just going to pull out your hand. Sorry. You're ending the live training. See you later, guys. Bye. Take care.
